results.query: How does the system handle peak load without dropping requests?
results.expanded_query: How does the system handle peak load without dropping requests? autoscaling rate limit queue depth load shedding

strategy_a.text: During peak load the system absorbs sudden traffic spikes by combining horizontal autoscaling with a short-lived queue. The queue depth triggers scale-out, while a token bucket rate limiter sheds excess requests to protect critical paths. A small percentage of best-effort traffic is dropped when the surge exceeds capacity.

Peak network load is different from CPU load. When bandwidth saturates, the mitigation focuses on CDN offload, response compression, and request batching instead of CPU autoscaling. Edge caching reduces origin fan-in during a flash crowd.

When downstream latency spikes, the API enforces timeouts and circuit breakers to prevent cascading failure. Backpressure is applied by bounding the in-flight request pool, and retries are capped with exponential backoff to avoid retry storms. Bulkheads isolate slow dependencies.

strategy_b.text: During peak load the system absorbs sudden traffic spikes by combining horizontal autoscaling with a short-lived queue. The queue depth triggers scale-out, while a token bucket rate limiter sheds excess requests to protect critical paths. A small percentage of best-effort traffic is dropped when the surge exceeds capacity.

If queue backlog grows faster than workers can drain, the system raises backpressure signals and temporarily disables expensive features. Overload control is triggered by sustained queue depth and rising processing time, not by single spikes.

Peak network load is different from CPU load. When bandwidth saturates, the mitigation focuses on CDN offload, response compression, and request batching instead of CPU autoscaling. Edge caching reduces origin fan-in during a flash crowd.

results.query: What mitigates a thundering herd after cache invalidation?
results.expanded_query: What mitigates a thundering herd after cache invalidation? stale-while-revalidate request coalescing singleflight

strategy_a.text: Cache invalidation can cause a thundering herd when many clients miss simultaneously. The mitigation is request coalescing (singleflight) plus stale-while-revalidate, which serves slightly stale content while one request recomputes the value. A jittered TTL reduces synchronized expirations.

metadata.title: Cache invalidation and thundering herd control

strategy_a.text: When downstream latency spikes, the API enforces timeouts and circuit breakers to prevent cascading failure. Backpressure is applied by bounding the in-flight request pool, and retries are capped with exponential backoff to avoid retry storms. Bulkheads isolate slow dependencies.

Batch processors rely on idempotent writes so retries do not duplicate side effects. A dead-letter queue captures poison messages and caps retry counts. This avoids runaway retries that can resemble a load spike during recovery.
